title: Hashmaps
description: Hash maps are the single most important data structure in interviews. Master both Map and plain objects for O(1) lookups, frequency counting, and complement patterns.

sections.title: Map vs plain object
sections.explanation: Use `new Map()` for interview code — it handles any key type, preserves insertion order, and has a clean API. Plain objects work for string keys but have prototype pitfalls. Map has `.size` instead of Object.keys().length. Interviewers prefer Map for clarity.

exercise.prompt: Create a Map, set key "x" to 10 and key "y" to 20. Print the value for "x", print the size, then print whether the map has key "y".

validation.message: get('x') returns 10, size is 2, has('y') is true
Use Map's set, get, has, and size API

exercise.hint: Use .set() to add entries and .size (not .length) for the count.

sections.title: set, get, has, and delete
sections.explanation: Map's core API is set/get/has/delete — all O(1) average. get() returns undefined for missing keys, so always check with has() first or use a default. delete() returns true if the key existed. These operations form the basis of nearly every hashmap interview pattern.

exercise.prompt: Create a Map with entries (1, "a"), (2, "b"), (3, "c"). Print has(2), then delete key 2, print has(2) again, and print the remaining size.

validation.message: has(2) is true before delete, false after, size drops to 2
Use delete to remove and has to check existence

exercise.hint: delete(2) removes the entry, then has(2) returns false.

sections.title: Iterating over a Map
sections.explanation: Maps are iterable with for...of. Use `map.entries()` for [key, value] pairs, `map.keys()` for keys, and `map.values()` for values. Destructuring in the loop is idiomatic: `for (const [key, val] of map)`. This pattern appears in frequency counting and grouping problems.

exercise.prompt: Create a Map from the array [["x", 10], ["y", 20], ["z", 30]]. Iterate with for...of and destructuring, printing each key:value pair on its own line.

validation.message: Each entry should print as key:value on its own line
Use for...of with destructuring to iterate the Map

exercise.hint: Destructure as [key, val] and print key + ":" + val.

sections.title: Frequency counting pattern
sections.explanation: The most common hashmap interview pattern: count occurrences of each element. Use `map.get(key) || 0` to handle missing keys, or `map.get(key) ?? 0` for null-safe default. This pattern is the foundation for anagram checks, majority element, and top-K problems.

exercise.prompt: Count character frequencies in "hello" using a Map. Then print each character and its count in insertion order, formatted as char:count, each on its own line.

validation.message: h, e each appear once, l appears twice, o appears once
Use Map with get/set for frequency counting

exercise.hint: Use || to default to 0 when the key doesn't exist: (freq.get(ch) || 0) + 1.

sections.title: Complement lookup pattern
sections.explanation: The complement pattern stores previously seen values to find pairs that satisfy a condition — the classic Two Sum approach. For each element, check if its complement exists in the map; if not, store the current element. This converts O(n^2) brute force into O(n) time.

exercise.prompt: Implement twoSum: given nums and target, return indices of two numbers that add to target. Use the complement pattern with a Map. Test with nums=[2, 7, 11, 15], target=9 and print the result joined by comma.

validation.message: Use Map's has/get for the complement lookup pattern

exercise.hint: complement = target - nums[i], then check seen.has(complement).
